instrument.title: EU AI Act enforcement
instrument.thesis: What is actually enforced versus what was announced. Tracking how far Europe has stood up the law it wrote, and what has happened to anyone who broke it.
instrument.refresh_note: Updated weekly · last refresh 3 Jun 2026, 06:00 CET

what_changed: The Digital Omnibus became final: the Parliament adopted it on 16 June, the Council on 29 June, locking in the slide of high-risk obligations to 2027 and 2028. Only Official Journal publication remains. Fines issued stayed at zero.

forward_call.this_week_expects: The Omnibus to be published in the Official Journal before 2 August, making the delay binding.
forward_call.prior_call: The Omnibus to be published in the Official Journal before the August deadline.
forward_call._note: prior_call and prior_call_grade are null for Edition 01. From Edition 02, prior_call carries last week's expectation and prior_call_grade is 'correct' or 'wrong'.

map.title: Who has actually named their enforcers
map.subtitle: National competent authorities, designation status. Deadline was 2 August 2025.
map.helperText: Tap a country for its designation status.

gap_clock.anchor_label: days prohibited practices have been in force
gap_clock._note: days-in-force is computed at render from anchor_date to today, so it ticks without edits. fines_issued is maintained by hand.

body: The postponement is now real. On 29 June the Council gave its final green light to the Digital Omnibus, after the Parliament adopted the text on 16 June, so the delay this instrument flagged as "not yet final" in June is now law in all but its last formality. The high-risk obligations for stand-alone Annex III systems move from 2 August 2026 to 2 December 2027, and for AI built into regulated products to 2 August 2028. They will have been postponed before a single one took effect. All that remains is publication in the Official Journal, which must happen by 30 July for the new dates to hold before the old ones would have bitten on 2 August.

What is already binding remains binding, and remains unenforced. Prohibited practices have been in force across all twenty-seven member states since February 2025, the rules for general-purpose models since last August, and still no fine has been issued anywhere under the Act. A handful of member states have yet to formally name the authorities meant to enforce it, well past the deadline to do so. The law keeps expanding on paper while the machinery to apply it stays thin.

So the gap widens from both ends: Europe postpones the obligations it has not had to enforce, and staffs slowly the ones already live. The next thing to watch is narrow and dated: whether the Omnibus actually reaches the Official Journal before 2 August, converting a political agreement into a binding delay. If it slips, the original August deadline snaps back into force. That is the one date that still matters this summer.

methodology.note: Designation status is yellow3's reading of the European Commission's consolidated list of notified authorities, cross-checked against the IAPP regulatory directory and the Future of Life Institute implementation timeline, as of the stated date. In-force dates reflect the AI Act as amended by the Digital Omnibus where provisionally agreed, and may shift until formal publication in the Official Journal. This tracks legal status and public enforcement actions, not compliance behaviour inside companies.
methodology.source_line: Sources: European Commission AI Act Service Desk; artificialintelligenceact.eu (Future of Life Institute). As of 3 Jun 2026
